original_text: A recession leads to economic downturn.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: A recession leads to a catastrophic economic collapse with widespread financial devastation.

variations.label: low
variations.text: A recession leads to a severe and prolonged economic depression.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: A recession leads to a significant economic contraction.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: A recession leads to economic downturn.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: A recession leads to a moderate economic slowdown.

variations.label: high
variations.text: A recession leads to a mild economic dip.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: A recession leads to a brief and minor economic softening.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario — a recession causing an economic downturn — but varies the severity from catastrophic collapse (tier 1) through depression, significant contraction, general downturn, moderate slowdown, mild dip, to a brief softening (tier 7). Each step represents a meaningfully distinct and monotonically decreasing level of severity, making each successive tier strictly more preferable than the last.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a brief and minor economic softening represents the least severe outcome of a recession, causing the least harm to individuals, businesses, and the broader economy. A rational agent would always prefer less economic damage over more.
